figures.caption: Fig. 5. Curvature-adaptive sampling performance across maps. For this method, the sensitivity parameter α (how much curvature affects the waypoint spacing) is varied from 5 to 40 in increments of 2.5, while ds spans from 2m to 10m in increments of 1m. Color scales are shared between the two smoothing modes, centered-window (γ = 1) and forward-window (γ = 0), for direct comparison. The forward window consistently shifts performance toward better reliability and candidate yield; the top configurations under J are at γ = 0, ds = 10m, and moderate α ∈ {15, 17.5, 22.5}m. Annotation convention as in Fig. 3.
Fig. 1. Waypoint selection strategies: 1 Uniform sampling, 2
TABLE III TOP-10 CONFIGURATIONS RANKED BY J FOR THREE WEIGHT SCENARIOS.

Fig. 2. Systematic evaluation framework. (A) Scenario selection: five CommonRoad maps span road layouts of increasing geometric complexity. (B) Planning poses: from each map we extract a fixed-length corridor along the centerline and define elliptical sampling regions along it; within each ellipse we draw ego states with uniformly sampled positions (b.1) and Gaussian perturbations to heading and curvature (b.2), with curvature magnitude encoded from purple (|κ| = 0) to yellow (|κ| = 0.25). (C) Evaluation: for each ego state, waypoint configuration, and map, the trajectory generator is invoked once, yielding candidate trajectory sets from which we compute validity, length, and diversity metrics.

Fig. 4. RDP and RDP* performance across maps. Plain RDP (▲) and the augmented RDP* variant (■) are evaluated as a function of simplification tolerance ε, swept from 0.01 to 1.00 m; for RDP*, gap-filling is applied at ds ∈ {2, 3, 5, 7}m. Columns and color convention follow Fig. 3. Color scales are set independently for RDP and RDP* due to their markedly different metric ranges. RDP* consistently outperforms plain RDP across all metrics except trajectory length; within RDP*, the best configurations favor large ds and moderate to high ε, with the top three at ds = 7m and ε ∈ {0.35, 0.45, 0.50}m. Annotation follow the convention of Fig. 3: bold for best J , ⊕ for top-3, ⊖ for the minimum.

Fig. 3. Uniform sampling performance across maps. The waypoint separation distance ds is varied from 0.5m to 10m in increments of 0.25m. For each variation, columns report (left to right): total failed planning queries, mean valid candidates, mean trajectory length, and mean diversity; aligned with f1–f4, these contribute 70%, 20%, 8%, and 2% respectively to the weighted score J shown in the last column. Brighter cells indicate more desirable values. The bold entry marks the best J ; ⊕ indicates the top-3 configurations and ⊖ the minimum. A moderate spacing around ds ≈ 8m achieves the best balance between reliability and candidate yield.
